title: KitchenAid Blade Coffee Grinder - Onyx Black
description:
About this item
Clear Top Cover and One-Touch Control allows for operation at the touch of a button—simply push down on the cover to begin grinding and release to stop. The clear cover also allows you to easily see the consistency of grinds.
Stainless Steel Blade offers durability and powerful performance, quickly grinding enough whole coffee beans to make up to 12 cups of coffee.
Removable 4-Oz. Stainless Steel Bowl features measurement markings on the inside, so you know at a glace the quantity of coffee beans needed to make 4, 8, 10 or 12 cups of coffee. The bowl lifts out, making it easy to transfer grinds to the coffee maker.
Model BCG111 includes heavy duty motor housing, stainless steel 4-ounce capacity bowl with stainless steel coffee grinder blade, clear top cover.
1 year hassle-free replacement warranty.
reviews_5s:
Got this grinder yesterday - in use today. THE big improvment for me is the deep cup which holds the beans. Vendor claims it holds from level 4 (min) to 12 (max) of beans that will on average deliver 4-12 cups of coffee. After some use I can vouch for this. For optimal flavor, the experts insist you consume promptly after grinding. But, I like the convenience of grinding 8-12 cups worth early in the day, transfer to a handy jar, and we live off that for the rest of day. Flavor is good the whole time. Everything about this grinder is solid and well made, unlike others I have used. And, it's the quietest blade grinder I have ever used. It has that feel of a machine that will last a long time. I wont say more for now, but intend to return to update my review a month from now when I expect to know more.... Dec 13, 2020 UPDATE. I've been using this coffee grinder for at least a month now, and can confirm that the KitchenAid Grinder (BCG111ER) exceeds all my hopes and expectations, best grinder of the many I've had in the past several decades. Best features = (1) excellent control over the extent of grind (from fine to coarse). At max bean fill (that will make at least 12 cups), if you hold the lid down (i.e., turn the grinder on) for 20-25 seconds you get a fine grind suitable all the way to espresso. [Note - for espresso you may need to go longer than 25 seconds (with a full grinder cup) depending on your espresso machine and the nature of the beans. The vendor cautions that excessive grind time may over heat the grinder. As a test, I went up to 29 seconds without over heating my machine.] Between 15 to 20 seconds will give you a coarser grind suitable for example for a french press (mine works best at 16 seconds). Remember, I determined these grinder times required to get my desired grind (coarse to fine) WITH THE GRINDER CUP FILLED TO MAXIMUM WITH COFFEE BEANS. If you put fewer beans in the grinder cup, required time to get a particular grind will be reduced. (2) Consistency of grind - For a given quantity of beans and a set grind time I get very much the same grind output every time. (3) Small foot print. This KitchenAid grinder takes up very little space on your counter top. (4) Sturdy build that makes below average noise, and feels solid with a pleasing heft to it.
To get the performance you want, you should go through a bench-marking process similar to what I did so you can consistently get the extent of grind you prefer (coarse to fine). You really need a timer for this. And, for ease of use, you should carefully familiarize yourself with the outer (clear plastic) cap and the stainess steel inner cup which holds the beans. Notice that the outer cap connects to the grinder body with two small plastic pins that extend down from this cap and fit into two corresponding holes in the grinder body. These pins have to fit into their holes or you can not push the cap down far enough to start the grinder motor. This is a safety feature. God forbid that a child could somehow start the ginder. And, for someone who is sometimes a bit rough with their equipment, this 'pin in the hole' design may also present a slight chance of breaking one of the plastic pins. In that event, you will need another plastic cap and I don't know if KitchenAid sells replacement parts.
Another safety feature is having the cutting blade built into the steel cup which holds the beans. If the cup is not inserted into the grinder body, it might be possilbe to start the motor (I didn't try this), but only the driver shaft would spin harmlessly, there being no blade to engage. There are indentations on the cup which must be aligned with an obvious mark on the grinder body. To accomplish this, insert the cup with the indentation aligned. It wont fit in! Good, that's another safety feature. While gently urging the cup to insert downward, rotate the cup counter clockwise. After a half inch or so, the cup will readily drop down to the 'engagement level'. At this lower level, rotate the cup back in a clockwise move until that cup indentation is aligned as desired. The fit is snug, another reflection of the grinder's sturdy build.Here's a five-star review for the KitchenAid Blade Coffee Grinder:

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Compact, Powerful, and Perfect for Fresh Coffee Every Morning!

This KitchenAid Blade Coffee Grinder is a total game-changer for my coffee routine! It’s compact but powerful, grinding my coffee beans to perfection in just seconds. I love that I can adjust the grind by simply controlling how long I press the button, allowing me to achieve anything from coarse to fine grounds depending on my brewing method.

The stainless steel blade is sharp and efficient, and the removable stainless steel bowl makes cleaning super easy. Plus, it’s perfect for measuring the exact amount of coffee I need, with convenient markings that ensure consistency every time. The grinder itself is also very quiet compared to others I’ve used, which is a huge plus in the mornings.

Overall, this grinder is sleek, efficient, and delivers freshly ground coffee with minimal effort. It’s sturdy and built to last, which I expect from KitchenAid. For anyone looking to upgrade their coffee experience, this grinder is a must-have. Highly recommend!I had many grinders over the years that didn't perform well until this KitchenAid caught my eyes online! I ordered the combo right away. Out of the box, the grinder is made with high quality shiny sliver polycarbonate/stainless materials. The combo pack comes with (1) Burr grinder, (1) Coffee grind bowl, (2) Spice grind bowl, (1) Shaker lid, (2) Airtight storage lid. It is easy to use and clean, quit, and grind coffee beans and other dry spices effortlessly. Highly recommend!!Got this to grind coffee beans at home for my coffee. This machine is quite powerful and can grind a lot of beans or spices at once and it can get down to a very fine texture. the noise depends on what you grinding. Super simple to use.Perfect for grinding coffee, it is very fast and quiet, in a matter of 10 seconds we have fresh, delicious ground coffee!!!I did quite a bit of research before purchasing this coffee mill. Even the expensive brands seemed to have poor reviews and the two main complaints in those reviews (not grinding all the beans and leaving chunks, and being too messy because there was not a removable cup) are NOT an issue with this model as far as I can tell. I've owned this about three weeks and couldn't be more pleased, in fact it exceeded every expectation. When I saw the blades for the first time, I assumed it was going to also leave some chucks of unground beans in the receptacle. Never has it done that. The grind is perfectly even and fine. I was also surprised that it is relatively quiet when grinding. This unit is VERY easy to clean and not messy because the receptacle lifts out and you empty that into the coffee filter rather than turning over the whole unit. Plus, both the grinding receptacle and the lid can be washed so you could easily switch from grinding coffee beans one day to grinding some peppercorns the next. You could never do that with a coffee grinder that didn't have a lift-out receptacle that you could throw in the dishwasher because there would be residual flavor in the receptacle. I have not tried grinding spices with it, but judging on how well it works with whole coffee beans I think it would do fine. I expected to like this unit, but thought I would have some of the same issues that other folks had experienced like the receptacle being somewhat difficult to lift out, or the lid getting covered with grounds. Honestly, each time I have used it the receptacle has been a piece of cake to lift out, I've had zero trouble with that. And even when I fill the receptacle full to grind a lot of beans I have yet to get the clear lid dirty with grinds. I realize I'm praising this thing to the point where it sounds perfect, but since I love it so far I really can't think of a negative. It's attractive on the counter too.

Update June 16, 2012 : Still 100% happy with this purchase. Even when I fill the reservoir full with beans and grind a lot at once, it never leaves any unground beans. Not even a small chunk remains, there is just a perfectly smooth, fine grind. And it grinds a full reservoir extremely fast (in mere SECONDS). I've put the removable reservoir in the dishwasher several times so far, no problem. I don't wash it every day. I wash it about once a week. I've never had loose grounds on the countertop and it's never made a mess, so I'm unsure why some reviews seem to say it made a mess. I'm being fully honest that I cannot think of a single thing that I dislike about this appliance. P.S. I don't put the clear acrylic top in the dishwasher. It's never dirty so I've never washed the lid, just the stainless steel bean reservoir.Probably my favorite coffee grinder I've found. Easy to use, just lift the plastic lid off, pour in some beans, replace the lid, and press down.

A few grounds may be stuck in the cup when you go to pour but a soft tap on the side or a spin of the wheel from the back releases the grounds.

Also be careful when unscrewing the grinder portion, the edge of the metal cup can be a little sharp. Unscrewing from the side shouldn't give you any problems.I bought the his great coffee b a grinder for Christmas. She loves it. She says it works great. It arrived in two days. Great vendor. I gladly give them a five star review . Thank you.Purchased for my mother for Christmas she loved it works great and nice color.Se los recomiendo al 100 la mejor para moler especias
reviews_1s:
Play Video
No that’s not the Muddy Buddy ice cream or what ever it’s called. That’s half horribly ground goodness.

Maybe this is the way grinders use to be, but even the generic brands you can pronounce, let alone recognize, are WAYYYYYY better. Don’t waste your money.

First, it tells you for 12 cups it takes 21 seconds… the cheap one I had before only took 12 seconds! Didn’t break, someone needed it and knew it was easy for me to replace. Figured the KitchenAid grinder would be awesome since it’s a big household name… Wrong…

Second, it only ground dead middle and never grabbed the outer rim to pull the beans down! So now I have to stop it, remove top and mix or shake it… the cheap one would grind it perfectly??? So it’s still ground unevenly! Even if you kept going thinking it would grab the middle and mix, the middle would be powder and you’d still have larger pieces throughout. I ground one 12 cup portion, per their container markings, for 30+ seconds to see if that would do the trick… Nope! I had planned on throwing that batch away, but needed to see if it was just me.

Third, it comes with NO brush or measuring spoon. Those two, especially a brush, always come with a grinder! I get that the cup has measurements on it, but you don’t exactly grab the beans with your hands and throw them into the cup!

I will have to say it is quite! However considering it’s not really grinding anything that may be the reason. Think I’ll take 12 seconds of louder noise to make sure it’s good, and not 30 minutes of quieter grinding!

Now I have a funky grind. Lord knows what it’s gonna taste like, but gonna order a generic cheap grinder asap and pray I get it by tomorrow morning!

Of course I gotta deal with going out of my way to return this one to get the refund. Save yourself time and money, skip it!

Update: I would like to note that I am using Starbucks mild, so it’s not garbage beans that’s the issue…

After trying the 30 second grind to see if it would work, I dumped that and started again to get actual coffee going… Lose pack 12 cups in the grinder, and held it for their recommended 21 seconds. When trying to dump it I got that… It was the consistency of powdered Cocoa, and there was a cavern under neath. Again… 12 cups, 21 seconds. Followed THIER directions to the T. Go shower, come back, and notice there’s 3/4 of the pot that’s missing.

I open up the top to find that lovely mess!!!!!! I am SOOOO ANGRY!!!! It overflowed that powdery coffee grind back into the water tank. There’s no cleaning that. The coffee powder, AND OIL are now in or on the tank, pump, heater, etc. NOW I have to get another coffee maker because of this garbage grinder? Thanks KitchenAid. I had a dishwasher made by you that was sudo ok, but now I see it’s getting effective “better” with your quality!!!!!I bought this grinder to replace our old KitchenAid grinder which I loved. It lasted about 15 years and probably would’ve lasted another 15 if the hard rubber piece inside the base that spins the blade didn’t break apart. Not sure when they stopped making the old design but this new one looks and feels completely different. If I had known this new one was going to be this bad I would have tried to fix the old one.
The biggest reason for my dislike (and I’m assuming it’s the same reason that you’re interested in buying a coffee grinder in the first place)—IT STINKS AT GRINDING COFFEE!!! You would think KitchenAid would make a grinder that grinds coffee beans evenly after all these years but not this thing. Some of the coffee beans come out finely ground while some of the beans come out whole—as in the same way they went into the grinder. In order for the beans to come out somewhat evenly ground, I would have to grind for a few seconds, then take the lid off and stir the grinds with the back end of a spoon and repeat three or four times.
Finally it hit me why it’s so useless. LOOK AT THE BLADE!!! (scroll through the manufacturer’s pictures until you see inside the cup). Both ends of the blade are angled up. No wonder beans settle at the bottom without getting touched. A few people have alluded to this problem but I didn’t see a review that addressed the reason directly. IT’S SO OBVIOUS!!! Every grinder I’ve owned previously had an opposing angled blade (one side of the blade is angled up, the other is down). I cannot imagine why anyone would give this grinder five stars.
After a few months of frustration, I bought a $15 Toastmaster grinder from Ocean State Job Lot and would give it ten stars compared to the KitchenAid grinder. I made sure the blade was angled correctly and sure enough, perfectly even grinding.
Other reasons why I don’t like the KitchenAid grinder…..it’s loud and it makes a mess. Coffee grinds seem to find their way into the base (exactly where the last one broke), as well as all over the counter.
The $15 Toastmaster is quieter, cleaner and feels more solid. Although it doesn’t have a removable cup, I’m OK with that because that’s where the last KitchenAid broke. On the bright side, this new KitchenAid grinder will probably last many, many years….PROBABLY BECAUSE I’M NEVER GOING TO USE IT AGAIN!The grinder is aesthetically pleasing and not something I would expect to be returned. I liked the removable cup so that I could dump the product easily after it was ground up. Overall, however, the grinder was not very usable for me. The lid was not all that easy to line up and get it into operational mode to press down and grind. The grinder was loud and created a dust of dry product that escaped the cup and around the lid and ended up all over the place. After I realized that it left chunks in the cup after persistently trying to get all of it ground up, it ultimately left much to be desired.Tried two of these hoping for better results the second time around. Ultimately we returned both. There are a few limited "pros". It's nice looking - but with some caveats. The base is uniformly round (to my eyes the photo suggests four sides). The "black" model is actually silver with some black trim. And the red one we received appeared brighter red than in the photo. Notwithstanding these differences in perception, it's still an attractive item. Cord hides away inside, a nice feature. However, even when fully extended it measures only 30". Cost is attractive. Both purchases came in at $22.50, while the "list price" shown on Amazon is about double that.

We make a lot of coffee. A carafe every morning (drip brewed). Fresh decaf after lunch and another in the evening. Not looking for perfection here - just something not harsh or bitter tasting. The grinding chamber on this unit is somewhat narrow and taller than typical. That may be why the blades ride a bit higher. Not being a mechanical engineer, I can't say why the grind is consistently very coarse - but it seems to be. A worn and tattered $15-$20 B&D we pulled out of storage ran circles around this KA for speed, grind and ultimate coffee flavor.

Grinding longer than 25 seconds at a time would exceed the time-limit in their instructions - conceivably jeapordizing the warranty. Those instructions, found inside the box, also tell you not to pulse. I tried shaking, but the cord is pretty stiff and on the short side so pulled loose repeatedly from the wall. I don't think shaking helped. Nor did varrying the type or amount of beans make much difference. Am not entirely convinced here that coarseness alone was responsible for the poor flavor experienced. There are many other factors one can't easily measure, including heat generated during grinding process and the exact cut and consistency of grind produced. I'll say that this was some of the worst coffee I've tasted. And not for lack of trying.

Also plan on doing a lot of scraping, as any attempt to grind finely will result in dense packing of the coffee. More coarse grinds also tend to stick and need to be scraped out. How did this grinder attain all the high ratings? Perhaps it's the good looks, low price and the convenience of the removable cup. (But oh - the awful taste.) We incurred an $8 return shipping charge on each of the two units we decided to return, as we did not consider these defective - just poorly designed.This grinder is useless in my opinion. The grind in VERY uneven. I put in one scoop of beans and the beans on top were barely ground and what was on the bottom was like powder ( last 2 pics). Needless to say my cup of coffee (brewed in my moka pot) from the grounds this thing kind of geound, wasn't as good as normal. This grinder takes a long time to grind. But one of the worst things is the cup and the plastic lid do not meet to seal in the grounds and they just fly around all over the place and make a mess as well as waste. I also dislike the metal cup. It isn't easy to get in and out of the grinder base and the top is a bit sharp to grab but the body feels like I will bend it if I try to grip it. I dislike this grinder and would give it zero stars if that was an option. I was surprised that this big name would perform so poorly. My husband said it's garbage and I agree. I would caution against this grinder as it is a waste of money and basically useless. I will go back to the same brand grinder I had before I mistakenly purchased this one. (I used my mueller grinder at least twice a day for 4 years. It worked perfectly but it just started to make a funny noise and at the price I paid and how well that one performed, it is well worth replacing - Mueller). This kitchen aid grinder is being returned!!KitchenAid has a great reputation - I have many of their products, but I will not gift a filthy item. Yes - it arrived the way it’s pictured in the photos. There is no option for me to comment on the seller or packaging - only a link to comment on the product. This product came from the Amazon Prime warehouse, not a third party seller. It was supposed to be new - not used or refurbished - but clearly it was previously used and returned to the warehouse shelves for sale.This unit is attractive but has a design flaw which keeps it from grinding coffee properly. The grind is uneven, producing some large chunks which come to the top plus a quantity of very fine powder remaining stuck under the blades at the bottom of the cup. The powder tends to get jammed there and will not come out by itself, even if one taps the side of the cup. It must be dug out with a finger, cautiously so as not to cut oneself on blades, or using some implement. Annoying. Other Amazon buyers have noted the same problem.

One star and a down vote to the product people who put this on the market without careful testing. This is the sign of a company which is run by the marketing types, not the engineers. Probably all they cared about was how it looked from the outside. No integrity.

OK, I am now going to tell you how I MODIFIED this grinder to do a much better job. Now if the KA management gets wind of this, they will have a heart attack and call a weekend meeting of their legal staff, so please keep this just among us Amazonians. Promise? Here's what you do. The problem with the little blade is that the tips on both ends turn UP; you want one of the tips to turn DOWN. So bend one tip down. Simply find a bit of steel of the right thickness from your parts bin to fit under the blade exactly, and using this as a fulcrum, use a steel rod to push down on the tip on that end until it bends down instead of up. Don't push it too far or it will scrape the housing as it rotates. However, the blade is cheap Chinese steel and it doesn't take much force to bend it. That's all. You now have a grinder blade which turns up at one end and down at the other. You will see this works much better, creates a much more uniform particle size, and won't capture or trap powdery stuff under the blade. If the KA suits do read this, I will happily accept your $1M consulting fee for improving your product. Thanks.This is the worst product kitchenaid has ever made and it is a black eye to their brand. I have gone through 3-4 of these things in the last few years. I use it for grinding coffee. It does that one job well for about a year then the screw that whips rhe blade around strips out. In November 2021 I bought a brand new unit because it was $29 and the stainless steel cup by itself is $20. Here we are two years later and the screw is failing again. I still have a brand new unit sans cup sitting in my cupboard. I tried to order a replacement cup directly from kitchenaid and they notified me my order can't be filled and are refunding my money. So I have to spend another $30 for one of these POS grinders and there isn't really another alternative. Bur grinders with only one hopper won't work for me as I drink regular and decaf daily.

Overall, the durability of this thing is hamstrung by the screw in the cup - the motor is strong, the stainless steel didn't rust, the blade stays sharp. It's such a disappointment and waste. Every new appliance I buy lasts a year or two before it craps the bed.Grinds poorly.
Created random sized pieces while throwing grinds and grind dust all over inside and outside of the product.
I would send it back but I was called out of town on a family emergency and didn’t get back to use it until after the return date.
I expected more from a Kitchen Aid product.Save your $ and look elsewhere. They had a great concept with the smaller cups to use for spices, thus eliminating the need for separate grinders. But they blew it in the implementation. Firstly, the lid doesn't affix to the grinding cups, it just goes over them to trigger the start, so grinds end up outside the machine and all over around where the cups sit. Second, it doesn't grind uniformly and there's no settings for amount or grind size/type, you just guess based on counting seconds. But you end up with espresso grind at the bottom and cracked beans at the top. The cups are very thin walled as well, you can feel them flex inward when you grip them to twist them out of the base. Overall, this is a terrible machine!
